ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for tonic water as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for beverages of this type is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 330 ml can of tonic water, equivalent to approximately 330 g, is a typical single-serve portion consumed as a mixer or standalone beverage.

nutrient_source.title: Tonic Water — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00011060 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this canonical HPB record. USDA FoodData Central (NDB No. 14400, Carbonated beverage, tonic water) corroborates the macronutrient profile: energy ~34 kcal/100 ml, carbohydrate ~8.8 g/100 ml, protein 0 g, fat 0 g; the HPB figure of 84 kJ (approximately 20 kcal) per 100 g is consistent with a lower-sugar Singapore market formulation and is accepted as the primary source.

gi_source.title: Soft drink, regular (carbonated, sugar-sweetened) — GI analogous estimate based on sucrose-sweetened carbonated beverage data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for tonic water exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The closest defensible analogue is a sucrose-sweetened carbonated soft drink (GI approximately 59–63, mean 60) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Tonic water shares the same sugar type (sucrose or HFCS), similar sugar concentration (~6.6 g/100 g in this HPB formulation), and carbonated liquid matrix. GI is estimated at 60 (medium). Glycemic load per 330 g serving = (60 × 21.7 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 13.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage — added sugar at 6.57 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold; sodium is low at 9 mg/100 g but the sugar load promotes insulin-driven sodium retention and endothelial dysfunction.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid regular tonic water as a sugar-sweetened beverage and substitute diet (sugar-free) tonic water or plain sparkling water to eliminate the sugar-driven blood pressure risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: All carbohydrate (6.57 g/100 g) is free sugar with zero dietary fibre, producing a rapid glycaemic response; classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage under ADA and WHO guidelines.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid regular tonic water entirely; diet or sugar-free tonic water is the recommended substitute to prevent postprandial glucose spikes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased cardiovascular risk via dyslipidaemia, visceral adiposity, and elevated triglycerides; tonic water provides no cardioprotective nutrients.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit or eliminate regular tonic water and choose sugar-free alternatives to reduce added-sugar intake in line with AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar promotes pro-inflammatory cytokines; no anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols, fibre) are present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid regular tonic water as its added sugar content promotes systemic inflammation; plain sparkling water or diet tonic water is preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tonic water is a carbonated soft drink flavoured with quinine, citric acid, and natural flavourings, sweetened with sucrose or high-fructose corn syrup. The Singapore HPB Foodo database records this formulation at 84 kJ (approximately 20 kcal) per 100 g, with 6.57 g of carbohydrate per 100 g entirely as free sugar and zero dietary fibre, protein, or fat. It is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under ADA, WHO, and Singapore HPB dietary guidelines. Tonic water originated in British colonial India as a vehicle for quinine (an antimalarial), and the characteristic bitter taste from quinine remains its defining flavour note. It is widely consumed as a mixer in gin-and-tonic cocktails and as a standalone sparkling drink. Clinically, regular tonic water is contraindicated for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its free-sugar content and complete absence of fibre, micronutrients, or cardioprotective compounds. Diet (sugar-free) tonic water is a suitable substitute for those requiring carbohydrate restriction. In Singapore, tonic water is available in supermarkets and is used in both Western and fusion beverage contexts.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in tonic water?
Is tonic water a sugar-sweetened beverage?
Can I drink tonic water if I have high blood pressure?
What is quinine in tonic water?
Is tonic water suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database (cr_id G00011060, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). USDA FoodData Central corroborates the macronutrient profile for carbonated tonic water. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest approved analogue (sucrose-sweetened carbonated soft drink).
notes: Energy conversion: HPB reports 84 kJ per 100 g; converted to kcal as 84 / 4.184 = 20.1 kcal/100 g. protein_g is naturally zero — tonic water is a carbonated sugar-water beverage containing no protein by formulation; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central. fat_g is naturally zero — tonic water contains no fat by formulation; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g are all 0.0 as this is a fat-free beverage with no lipid fraction. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero — no fibre in a carbonated sugar-water beverage. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — plant-derived or synthetic beverage with no animal-derived ingredients. magnesium_mg is confirmed zero per HPB source. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because individual micronutrient and moisture data for this specific Singapore HPB tonic water formulation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database, and USDA FoodData Central (NDB No. 14400, tonic water) does not provide individual values for these nutrients beyond the macronutrient and selected mineral profile: vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Both approved sources (HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central) were consulted and confirmed that these micronutrient fields are not individually reported for carbonated tonic water; this is a standard data gap for ultra-processed carbonated beverages. Cultural note: input record classifies this as Chinese cuisine with SG region tag; tonic water is a globally manufactured carbonated beverage of British colonial origin and is not cuisine-specific, but the Singapore HPB source and SG regional tag are preserved. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context per G6 rules. GI analogous estimate: GI 60 derived from University of Sydney GI Database sucrose-sweetened carbonated soft drink analogue; GL per 330 g serving = (60 × 21.68) / 100 = 13.0.

Iteration 3: Expanded notes field to explicitly document every -1 sentinel value in per_100g, naming each field individually (vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g), stating the reason data is unavailable (not reported in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for carbonated tonic water), and citing both approved sources consulted that confirmed unavailability. This resolves the g3 hard gate failure from iteration 2.
